questions.question_text: Which protected class is covered by Wisconsin's Open Housing Law but NOT by the federal Fair Housing Act?
distractors: Race
Familial status
National origin
questions.explanation: Wisconsin's Open Housing Law adds several protected classes beyond federal law, including lawful source of income (Section 8 vouchers, Social Security), sexual orientation, marital status, age, ancestry, and domestic abuse victim status.

questions.question_text: A landlord refuses to rent to a tenant because they plan to pay with Section 8 vouchers. Under Wisconsin law, this is:
questions.correct_answer: Illegal discrimination based on lawful source of income
distractors: Legal because landlords can choose tenants
Illegal only in Milwaukee and Madison
Legal unless the property has 5+ units
questions.explanation: Wisconsin's Open Housing Law protects 'lawful source of income' as a protected class, which includes Section 8 vouchers, Social Security, and other government assistance. Refusing to rent based on this is illegal statewide.

questions.question_text: A landlord refuses to rent to a 20-year-old applicant solely because of their age. Under the Open Housing Law, this is:
questions.correct_answer: Illegal, age 18 and older is a protected class
distractors: Legal, age is not protected in Wisconsin housing
Illegal only if the applicant is over 40
Legal unless the property receives federal funding
questions.explanation: Wisconsin's Open Housing Law protects age as a class, covering anyone 18 years old and older. This is broader than federal employment law (which protects only 40+) and applies statewide to housing.

questions.question_text: A property manager declines to show an apartment to a couple because they are unmarried. Under Wisconsin law, this is:
questions.correct_answer: Illegal discrimination based on marital status
distractors: Legal because marital status is not protected
Illegal only under federal fair housing law
Legal unless the property receives federal funding
questions.explanation: Marital status is a protected class under Wisconsin's Open Housing Law. Refusing to rent or show property based on whether someone is married, single, or divorced is illegal in Wisconsin.

questions.question_text: Under Wisconsin's Open Housing Law, what is the minimum age for the 'age' protected class?
distractors: 21 years old
40 years old
No minimum age
questions.explanation: Wisconsin's Open Housing Law protects age as a class, defined as 18 and older. This prevents discrimination against younger adults (18+) in housing, unlike federal employment law which protects those 40+.

questions.question_text: Which state agency enforces Wisconsin's Open Housing Law?
questions.correct_answer: The Equal Rights Division of the DWD
distractors: The Department of Safety and Professional Services
The Real Estate Examining Board
The Attorney General's Office
questions.explanation: Wisconsin's Open Housing Law is enforced by the Equal Rights Division within the Department of Workforce Development (DWD). Complaints can also be filed with HUD or pursued through private lawsuits.

questions.question_text: Within what timeframe must a housing discrimination complaint be filed with the Equal Rights Division?
distractors: 180 days after the alleged discrimination
6 months after the alleged discrimination
2 years after the alleged discrimination
questions.explanation: A person alleging a violation under Wisconsin's Open Housing Law must file a complaint with the Equal Rights Division within 1 year of the alleged discrimination. The federal HUD complaint deadline is also 1 year.

questions.question_text: A renter believes their landlord violated the Open Housing Law. Which is NOT a remedy option?
distractors: Filing a complaint with the Equal Rights Division
Filing a private lawsuit in state court
Filing a complaint with HUD
questions.explanation: Enforcement options include filing with the Equal Rights Division, filing with HUD, or pursuing a private lawsuit. Remedies include damages, injunctive relief, and attorney fees. Housing discrimination is typically a civil matter, not criminal.

questions.question_text: What remedies are available under Wisconsin's Open Housing Law?
questions.correct_answer: Damages, injunctive relief, and attorney fees
distractors: Only monetary damages up to $10,000
Only injunctive relief, no damages
Criminal penalties and imprisonment
questions.explanation: The Open Housing Law provides for compensatory damages, injunctive relief (court orders to stop discrimination), and attorney fees. These remedies aim to make the victim whole and deter future violations.

questions.question_text: A person files a fair housing complaint with both the Equal Rights Division and HUD. Is this allowed?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, complaints may be filed with both agencies
distractors: No, only one agency at a time
Only if the complaint involves federal funds
Only for discrimination based on federal classes
questions.explanation: A complainant can file with the state Equal Rights Division and/or HUD and/or pursue a private lawsuit. Multiple avenues of enforcement are available and can be pursued simultaneously.

questions.question_text: A licensee practices in Madison. What must they understand about local fair housing protections?
questions.correct_answer: Madison may have additional protected classes beyond state law
distractors: Madison uses only federal fair housing law
Local ordinances cannot exceed state protections
Madison fair housing applies only to rentals
questions.explanation: Madison, Milwaukee, Dane County, and other Wisconsin municipalities may add protected classes beyond both federal and state law. Licensees must know the local ordinances for their area of practice.

questions.question_text: Which Wisconsin municipalities are known for having additional fair housing protections beyond state law?
distractors: Only Milwaukee has extra protections
Green Bay and Kenosha exclusively
No local jurisdictions add protections
questions.explanation: Madison, Milwaukee, Dane County, and other municipalities have adopted local fair housing ordinances that add protected classes beyond both federal and Wisconsin state law. Agents must stay current with local requirements.

questions.question_text: A licensee moves from rural Wisconsin to practice in Milwaukee. Regarding fair housing, they should:
distractors: Apply only federal fair housing standards
Assume state law covers all local requirements
Register their license with the city separately
questions.explanation: Milwaukee has local fair housing ordinances with additional protected classes beyond state and federal law. Licensees are responsible for knowing and complying with the fair housing laws in their area of practice.

questions.question_text: In Wisconsin, which level of government can add protected classes to fair housing law?
questions.correct_answer: Federal, state, and local governments all can
distractors: Only the federal government sets fair housing law
Only the state and federal government
Only municipalities with populations over 100,000
questions.explanation: Fair housing protections in Wisconsin operate at three levels: federal (Fair Housing Act), state (Open Housing Law), and local (municipal ordinances). Each level can add protections, creating a layered system.

questions.question_text: If a local ordinance protects a class not covered by state law, which standard applies to a licensee?
distractors: Only the state standard applies
The licensee chooses which to follow
Federal law supersedes all local rules
questions.explanation: When multiple fair housing laws apply, the most protective standard governs. A licensee must comply with all applicable laws — federal, state, and local — and the broadest set of protections prevails.
